coredata.dc:description: Magnesium alloys have significant advantages as biodegradable orthopedic implants. However, their rapid degradation in physiological environments and lack of efficient osteointegration severely limit clinical applications. To effectively regulate the degradation rate of ZK60 magnesium alloy and enhance its bone defect regeneration ability, this study employed micro-arc oxidation (MAO) and hydrothermal treatment to create a microporous coating, followed by a layered double hydroxide (LDH) coating to seal the micropores and enhance corrosion resistance. Meanwhile, polydopamine (PDA) modification and BMP2 loading were applied to construct a functional composite coating with both corrosion resistance and osteogenic activity. The corrosion resistance and bone defect regeneration performance were systematically evaluated in vitro and in vivo. Results showed that the LDH coating exhibited a typical layered structure and effectively sealed the MAO coating micropores, forming a dense protective layer. Electrochemical results showed that compared with pristine ZK60, the corrosion current density of BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60 decreased from 6.18 × 10−5 A/cm² to 8.15 × 10−6 A/cm², indicating the coating significantly retarded the corrosion rate. In vitro and in vivo biological assessments demonstrated that the coating effectively promoted rat bone marrow mesenchymal stem cells (BMSCs) adhesion and bone defect regeneration; specifically, the bone mineral density (BMD) value of the BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60 group was 454.25 ± 31.41 mg/cm³ , which was 1.44 times higher than that of the LDH/ZK60 group.This study provides a novel strategy for biodegradable magnesium alloys with excellent corrosion resistance and osteogenic potential.

head.ce:title: BMP2@PDA-LDH functionalized coating on ZK60 magnesium alloy: Enhanced corrosion resistance and promoted bone defect regeneration

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Magnesium alloys have significant advantages as biodegradable orthopedic implants. However, their rapid degradation in physiological environments and lack of efficient osteointegration severely limit clinical applications. To effectively regulate the degradation rate of ZK60 magnesium alloy and enhance its bone defect regeneration ability, this study employed micro-arc oxidation (MAO) and hydrothermal treatment to create a microporous coating, followed by a layered double hydroxide (LDH) coating to seal the micropores and enhance corrosion resistance. Meanwhile, polydopamine (PDA) modification and BMP2 loading were applied to construct a functional composite coating with both corrosion resistance and osteogenic activity. The corrosion resistance and bone defect regeneration performance were systematically evaluated in vitro and in vivo. Results showed that the LDH coating exhibited a typical layered structure and effectively sealed the MAO coating micropores, forming a dense protective layer. Electrochemical results showed that compared with pristine ZK60, the corrosion current density of BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60 decreased from 6.18 × 10−5 A/cm² to 8.15 × 10−6 A/cm², indicating the coating significantly retarded the corrosion rate. In vitro and in vivo biological assessments demonstrated that the coating effectively promoted rat bone marrow mesenchymal stem cells (BMSCs) adhesion and bone defect regeneration; specifically, the bone mineral density (BMD) value of the BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60 group was 454.25 ± 31.41 mg/cm³ , which was 1.44 times higher than that of the LDH/ZK60 group.This study provides a novel strategy for biodegradable magnesium alloys with excellent corrosion resistance and osteogenic potential.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Degradable metallic bone implants represent a significant advancement in the field of orthopedic implants, as their core advantage lies in avoiding the need for secondary surgery to remove traditional inert metal implants, thereby reducing patient pain and economic burden [1,2]. Magnesium (Mg) and its alloys, in particular, are considered a new generation of bone implants with great application potential due to their good biocompatibility, mechanical properties matching human bone, and degradable and absorbable characteristics in vivo. Moreover, the Mg²⁺ produced during degradation can directly participate in bone metabolism and cellular signaling processes [3−6]. Specifically, ZK60 magnesium alloy, a Mg-Zn-Zr alloy, possesses excellent mechanical strength, making it a research hotspot in the field of degradable bone implants [7−9].

However, the clinical application of magnesium and its alloys (including ZK60) faces a core contradiction. On one hand, an ideal bone implant scaffold should have a moderate degradation rate to ensure it maintains the necessary mechanical support during the critical period of bone tissue healing. On the other hand, magnesium alloys often degrade too quickly in physiological environments, leading to premature loss of structural integrity of the implant and an unfavorable situation where the rate of bone regeneration is lower than the rate of scaffold degradation. This may not only cause internal fixation failure but also hinder the bone regeneration process. These factors collectively limit the application of magnesium-based scaffolds in the field of biological implantation [10−13]. To address this contradiction, surface modification technology is considered one of the most effective methods. Its goal is to construct functional barriers on the scaffold surface to regulate degradation behavior while endowing it with positive biological functions [14−16]. Specifically, various anti-corrosion techniques, such as anodic oxidation, micro-arc oxidation and alloying, as well as the application of organic composite coatings and LDH coatings, can effectively enhance corrosion resistance [17−26]. Our early research has shown that hydrothermal treatment can effectively improve the corrosion resistance of ZK60 magnesium alloy [27]. Nevertheless, this study only adopted a one-pot hydrothermal method, focusing primarily on enhancing corrosion resistance and basic bioactivity, without involving the construction of composite coatings or the optimization of multi-functional synergy. In fact, single surface modification techniques often struggle to balance corrosion resistance and bioactivity. Constructing multilayer composite coatings with synergistic functions has become a frontier direction in current research. First, coatings with high bonding strength are crucial for regulating the long-term degradation rate of Mg scaffolds [28]. In recent developments, MAO coatings have attracted much attention due to their excellent bonding strength, as this technology can in-situ grow a micron-scale porous ceramic layer that bonds firmly to the alloy substrate [29−31]. Zewen Shi et al. found that by adjusting the voltage during the MAO process, the coating bonding strength reached 44.67 ± 3.22 MPa at 420 V, which is significantly higher than the commercial standard of 20 MPa [31]. This layer not only provides initial corrosion protection but also offers a stable adhesion interface for the subsequent coating deposition. Second, to address the drawback that the porous structure of the MAO layer may serve as rapid penetration channels for corrosive media [4,32−34], hydrothermal treatment is applied to grow an in situ LDH coating on the surface of the porous layer. LDH coatings are usually prepared via hydrothermal treatment in M³ ⁺ (metal ion)-containing alkaline solutions [35], commonly using Al³ ⁺ and Fe³ ⁺. However, Al has potential neurotoxicity and lacks beneficial biological functions, making it unsuitable for biomedical applications [36]; in contrast, Fe, an essential human trace element, participates in collagen synthesis, angiogenesis, and cellular energy metabolism to facilitate bone defect repair [37,38], and possesses both "biodegradability" and "biocompatibility" with superior safety. Therefore, Mg-Fe LDH is regarded as safe and applicable biomedical materials [39]. Its dense nanosheet structure can effectively physically seal the pores of the MAO layer, blocking the penetration paths of corrosive media, and further enhance the long-term protective ability of the coating by capturing corrosive anions (such as Cl⁻) [40]. In addition, the unique layered structure of the LDH coating makes it an ideal nanocarrier, capable of loading and delivering functional ions, drugs, and corrosion inhibitors, providing an excellent platform for further functional modification of the scaffold [41,42].

While addressing the degradation issue, endowing implants with the ability to actively promote osseointegration is crucial for enhancing their clinical efficacy. BMP2 is recognized as one of the most potent osteogenic inductive factors to date.

Seo-Young Kim et al. demonstrated that by loading different concentrations of BMP2 (20 ng/mL, 50 ng/mL, 100 ng/mL) onto the LDH coating of magnesium alloy, the osteogenic activity of the scaffold gradually increased with increasing BMP2 concentration [43]. However, its direct in vivo application is hindered by challenges such as difficult dosage control and susceptibility to dilution and loss [44−47]. Therefore, developing a surface system capable of stably loading BMP2 and achieving its controlled release is of great significance. PDA has been widely used for surface coating modification of various scaffolds due to its excellent adhesiveness, good hydrophilicity, and biocompatibility [6,7,48]. Our previous studies have confirmed that PDA can form a robust coating on the scaffold surface. Its molecular structure is rich in various active functional groups, enabling efficient immobilization of biomacromolecules such as BMP2 and realizing their long-term sustained release [49].

In this study, we developed a novel composite coating on a ZK60 substrate. We also systematically evaluated the corrosion resistance and osteogenic promotion capability of this material. This research provides a new strategy for developing magnesium-based biodegradable bone implant scaffolds with excellent mechanical support properties and high osteogenic activity.

Commercial ZK60 magnesium alloy (Kangji Magnesium Industry Co., Ltd., China) was used. For in vitro cellular experiments, 10 mm × 10 mm × 1 mm sheets were employed; 2 mm × 2 mm × 8 mm cylindrical scaffolds were used for in vivo experiments. Prior to surface modification, the scaffolds were sequentially mechanically polished with SiC papers up to 2000 grit, alternately rinsed with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol, dried, and labeled as the ZK60 group.

MAO coatings were prepared using a CNC dual-pulse power supply (YS9000DDB-70020, Shanghai Yisheng Electronic Technology Co., Ltd., China), with ZK60 as anode and stainless steel as cathode. The electrolyte consisted of 0.1 mol/L Na₂SiO₃⋅9H₂O and 0.1 mol/L NaOH. Constant voltage oxidation was performed at 600 Hz with a positive voltage of 300 V, a negative voltage of 20 V, and an oxidation duration of 600 s. The resulting scaffolds (MAO group) were thoroughly rinsed with methanol, absolute ethanol, and deionized water successively to remove residual electrolyte, and then air-dried at room temperature.

A mixed aqueous solution of 0.1 mol/L Mg(NO₃)₂·6H₂O and 0.05 mol/L Fe(NO₃)₃·9H₂O was prepared, and its pH was adjusted to 10 using 1 mol/L NaOH solution. The MAO-treated scaffolds were fully immersed in the above mixed solution and subjected to hydrothermal reaction at 100 °C for 24 h in a high-pressure hydrothermal autoclave (CY-03628, Xi’an Changyi Co., Ltd., China). The resulting scaffolds were labeled as the LDH/ZK60 group.

Sterilized LDH/ZK60 scaffolds were immersed in 2 mg/mL dopamine (DA) solution (dissolved in Tris-HCl buffer, pH 8.5) for 5 h to form a PDA coating. After rinsing three times with deionized water and air-drying, scaffolds were labeled as the PDA-LDH/ZK60 group.

PDA-LDH/ZK60 scaffolds were immersed in 500 ng/mL BMP2 (TMPY-01136, TargetMol, USA) solution and incubated at 4 °C overnight on a shaker for BMP2 immobilization (BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60 group). Scaffolds were subsequently washed, dried, UV-sterilized, and packaged. The preparation process is illustrated in Figure. 1.

The microstructures and thickness of coatings were observed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi SU8100, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 5 kV under vacuum conditions. Elemental compositions of the scaffold surfaces were analyzed using energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) attached to the SEM system.The chemical composition of the scaffold surfaces was examined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB25OXI, China). The crystal structure of the scaffolds was investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD, D8 ADVANCE, Germany) equipped with Cu Kα radiation. The scanning range was from 10 ° to 90 ° with a scanning rate of 2°·min−1. Surface roughness was measured using an atomic force microscope (AFM, Oxford Instruments Asylum Research, USA). Surface hydrophilicity was evaluated using a contact angle measuring instrument (SL200KS, Shanghai Suolun Information Technology Co., Ltd., China) with deionized water as the testing medium. Coating-substrate bonding strength (Anton Paar UNHT, Austria) and elastic modulus (Bruker HYSITRON TI 980, Germany) were measured by nanoindentation/scratch tester.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and polarization curves were recorded using an electrochemical analyzer (Princeton P4000, USA) in 300 mL simulated body fluid (SBF) with the following composition: 8.035 g/L NaCl, 0.355 g/L NaHCO₃, 0.225 g/L KCl, 0.231 g/L K₂HPO₄, 0.311 g/L MgCl₂·6H₂O, 0.292 g/L CaCl₂, 0.072 g/L Na₂SO₄, 6.118 g/L Tris.The electrochemical test was conducted using a three-electrode system with a 1 cm² exposure area as the working electrode, a saturated calomel electrode as the reference electrode, and a platinum (Pt) sheet as the counter electrode. Prior to testing, scaffolds were immersed in SBF for 1800 s to stabilize open circuit potential (OCP). EIS tests were performed at 0.1 Hz–100 kHz (10 points per decade) with 5 mV sinusoidal perturbation (analyzed by ZView software). Polarization curves were obtained at a scan range of −250–250 mV and scan rate of 1 mV/s. The corrosion current density was determined by the method of intersection of the cathodic branch of the potentiodynamic polarization curve with corrosion potential. The values of icorr (mA·cm−2) can be converted to the corresponding corrosion rate in the form of Pi (mm·y−1) according to the following formula:Pi = 22.85Icorr [50,51]. Each measurement was run more than three times.

Initial masses of scaffolds were recorded after cleaning and drying. Five groups (n = 3 per group) were immersed in PBS (pH 7.4) at 37 °C for 21 days in accordance with the ISO 10993–12 standard. After immersion, corrosion products were removed by immersing the scaffolds in a solution containing 1% AgNO₃ and 20% CrO₃ for 10 min [17]; scaffolds were rinsed, dehydrated, dried to constant weight, and reweighed. Corrosion rate was calculated as [50]:WL = (Wb − Wa)/(A × tL) (WL: weight loss rate, mg·cm⁻²·d⁻¹; Wb/Wa: mass before/after immersion, mg; A: surface area, cm²; tL: immersion time, days), and corrosion penetration rate as PW = 2.10 × WL (mm·y⁻¹). PBS pH was measured (n = 3) with fresh PBS replaced after each measurement.

For magnesium ion release analysis, five groups were immersed in PBS at 37 °C for 1, 3, 7, 21, and 42 days. Supernatants were collected at each time point (fresh PBS replenished), and magnesium ion concentrations were measured by inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES, Agilent 720ES, USA).

Cleaned scaffolds (five groups) were incubated with fetal bovine serum at 37 °C for 24 h. Adsorbed proteins were eluted with 1% sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS, Solarbio, China) and quantified using a BCA protein assay kit (YC373312, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) to compare protein adsorption capacities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In Vitro release assay of BMP2
ce:section.ce:para: BMP2 in vitro release was evaluated via enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA, Lunchangshuo Biotechnology Co., Ltd., China). BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60 scaffolds were placed in EP tubes with 1 mL PBS (full submersion) and incubated on a constant-temperature shaker. PBS was fully collected and replenished at 1, 2, 3, 5, 7, 14, and 21 days. All collected supernatants were frozen, and BMP2 concentration was determined later following the ELISA kit instructions.

Bone marrow mesenchymal stem cells (BMSCs) were isolated from femurs/tibias of 4-week-old male SD rats (Animal Center of Jiangxi University of Traditional Chinese Medicine). After cervical dislocation euthanasia and 75% ethanol disinfection, femurs/tibias were dissected, and bone marrow cavities were flushed with DMEM/F12 medium to collect cell suspensions. Cells were centrifuged, resuspended, seeded, and cultured at 37 °C with 5% CO₂ (medium changed every 3 days). P2 and P3 BMSCs were used for experiments.For different biocompatibility assays, cell seeding densities were adjusted according to specific experimental requirements to ensure the reliability and accuracy of results.

Scaffold extracts were prepared per ISO 10993–12. Complete medium was added to scaffolds and cultured at 37 ℃ with 5% CO₂ for 24 h. Extracts were membrane-sterilized, diluted to 50% working concentration with complete medium, aliquoted, and stored at 4 ℃.

BMSCs (4 × 10⁴ cells/mL) were seeded on scaffolds of all five groups and cultured for 24 h, then stained with Calcein-AM/PI kit (Servicebio, Wuhan, China) and observed via fluorescence microscopy (CKX53, OLYMPUS, Japan): live cells exhibited green fluorescence, while dead cells exhibited red fluorescence. For further validation, BMSCs were seeded in plates, cultured with extracts for 24 h, stained similarly, and analyzed by flow cytometry (Cytomics FC 500, Beckman Coulter, USA) to determine live/dead cell proportions.

Scaffold extracts were prepared per ISO 10993–12. Five groups were immersed in physiological saline at 37 ℃ for 30 min; supernatants were collected. SD rat orbital venous blood was drawn to isolate red blood cells (RBCs), which were prepared into 4% suspensions with extracts, physiological saline (negative control), and distilled water (positive control). Suspensions were incubated at 37 ℃ for 2 h, centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min, and supernatant absorbance was measured at 545 nm. Hemolysis rate (HR) was calculated as HR (%) = (ODsample − ODnegative)/(ODpositive − ODnegative) × 100%.

BMSCs (4 ×10⁴ cells/mL) were seeded on LDH/ZK60, PDA-LDH/ZK60, and BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60 scaffolds and cultured for 4 and 24 h. Cells were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde (10 min), permeabilized with 0.5% Triton X-100 (5 min), stained with TRITC-conjugated phalloidin (actin) and DAPI (nuclei), and observed via fluorescence microscopy.

BMSCs (2 × 10⁴ cells/mL) were seeded in 24-well plates and cultured with extracts of the LDH/ZK60, PDA-LDH/ZK60, and BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60 scaffolds for 1, 3, 5, and 7 days. Cell proliferation was measured via CCK-8 kit (GlpBio, USA): CCK-8 working solution was added, incubated for 2 h, and optical density (OD) at 450 nm was measured.

Scratch assay: BMSCs (5 × 10⁵ cells/well) were cultured in 6-well plates to ≥ 90% confluency, scratched with a 200 μL pipette tip, and washed with PBS. Experimental groups were treated with 2% FBS-containing extracts of LDH/ZK60, PDA-LDH/ZK60, and BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60 scaffolds; control group received 2% FBS DMEM/F12 medium. Scratch images were captured at 0, 12, 24, 36, and 48 h, and wound healing rate was calculated via ImageJ software.

Transwell assay: BMSCs were resuspended in serum-free DMEM/F12 medium at 1 × 10⁵ cells/mL, and 100 μL was seeded into the upper chambers; lower chambers contained 600 μL 2% FBS extracts (experimental) or medium (control). After 24 h incubation, cells were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde (20 min), stained with 0.1% crystal violet (15 min), and non-migrated cells were wiped off. Migrated cells were counted in five random fields for statistical analysis.

BMSCs (4 × 10⁴ cells/mL) were seeded in 24-well plates and cultured with osteogenic induction medium (consisting of 50 μg/mL ascorbic acid, 10 mM β-glycerophosphate, and 10 nM dexamethasone) containing the extracts of LDH/ZK60, PDA-LDH/ZK60 and BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60 groups.On day 7, alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity was detected via BCIP/NBT kit (Absin, China) and semi-quantified via Beyotime kit (p0321S, China). On day 14, osteogenic mineralized nodules were qualitatively detected by Alizarin Red S (ARS) staining (Beyotime kit) and quantitatively analyzed via 10% cetylpyridinium chloride elution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transcription and expression of osteogenic genes
ce:section.ce:para: Real-time quantitative PCR (RT-qPCR) was used to assess osteogenic gene (Col1a1, OPN, RUNX2; Table S1) expression after 14 days of induction. Total RNA was extracted (Liji Bio, China), quantified via NanoDrop One ultra-micro UV-Vis spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), and reverse-transcribed to cDNA (Takara Bio, Japan). The cDNA was mixed with TB Green, ROXⅡ and gene-specific primers, and amplified via real-time PCR (ABI 7500, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) to analyze the expression of target osteogenic genes.

Western blotting quantified Col1a1, OPN, and RUNX2 expression after 14 days of induction. Total protein was extracted, quantified, separated, transferred, and blocked per the manufacturer’s instructions. Membranes were incubated with primary antibodies (target proteins: 1:1000, Proteintech, China; GAPDH: 1:50000, Beyotime, China) at 4 °C overnight, washed with TBST, and incubated with secondary antibody (1:1000, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) for 2 h. Protein signals were detected via chemiluminescent substrate (Thermo, USA) and analyzed using a multifunctional optical density imaging system (ChemiDoc MP, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of bone defect animal model and scaffold implantation
ce:section.ce:para: This study was approved by the Animal Ethics Committee of Nanchang University (Approval No. NCULAE-20240624001) and adhered to the Helsinki guidelines. Four-week-old male SD rats were randomly divided into three groups (n = 4 per group; LDH/ZK60, PDA-LDH/ZK60, BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60). Rats were anesthetized with 3% pentobarbital sodium (0.5 mL/kg, i.p.). A 5 mm lateral femoral incision was made, muscles were dissected to expose the femoral head, and a 2 mm × 8 mm defect was created. Scaffolds were implanted after saline irrigation, and wounds were sutured and disinfected.

At 8 weeks post-surgery, rats were euthanized; femoral specimens were fixed in 4% paraformaldehyde for 2 days and analyzed via micro-CT (NMC-200, Pingsheng Medical Technology, China) with parameters: FOV 100 mm, axial range 250 mm, scan speed 4 s per bed, pixel size 2 μm, spatial resolution < 7.5 μm. 3D reconstruction was performed via Recon software (ROI = implant region). Bone parameters including bone surface area to tissue volume ratio (BS/TV), bone volume fraction (BV/TV), and bone mineral density (BMD) were measured using the BoneAnalyze software.

At 2 weeks post-surgery, rats were injected with calcein (10 mg/kg, MCE). At 8 weeks, specimens were fixed in 4% PFA, dehydrated, embedded in resin, sectioned (200 μm), ground (20–30 μm), and imaged via confocal laser scanning microscope (STELLARIS 5, Leica, Germany). Samples were then stained with toluidine blue-basic fuchsin and imaged via CellSens software.

For systemic biocompatibility, heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney tissues were collected, fixed in paraformaldehyde for 48 h, dehydrated, paraffin-embedded, sectioned, stained, and scanned via Pannoramic MIDI 3D system (images collected via SlideViewer).

All experiments were performed in triplicate. Data (mean ± SD) were analyzed via GraphPad Prism 8.0. Intergroup comparisons were conducted via t-tests or one-way ANOVA. Significance was defined as *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, and ***p < 0.001.

Figures. 2(a-e) display the surface morphologies of ZK60, MAO, LDH/ZK60, PDA-LDH/ZK60 and BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60 scaffolds. The ZK60 scaffold surface is relatively smooth, while the MAO scaffold has uniformly distributed micropores. These micropores are fully sealed by petal-like LDH nanosheets on LDH/ZK60 scaffold, and PDA-LDH/ZK60 and BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60 show no significant morphological difference from LDH/ZK60, verifying that PDA and BMP2 introduction does not alter surface morphology after hydrothermal treatment. Figures. 2(f-j) show the cross-sectional morphologies: the MAO coating presents a porous cross-section, which is fully covered and sealed by nanosheets after LDH modification (consistent with surface observations), The inner layer retains micropores and cracks formed by micro-arc oxidation (Figures. 2h-j). The thickness of the LDH layer is approximately ∼3 μm. After further PDA modification, a uniform PDA layer (0.1–0.2 μm) was formed on the surface. EDS spectra (Figures. 2k-o) confirm that ZK60 mainly contains Mg, Zn and Zr elements; Fe appears in LDH/ZK60, PDA-LDH/ZK60 and BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60, indicating successful LDH coating preparation. The N atomic percentages of the three groups are 1.64%, 2.57% and 3.63%, respectively. The increased N content in PDA-LDH/ZK60 is attributed to PDA amino groups, and the further rise in BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60 derives from primary amine and amide bonds in BMP2, proving successful loading of PDA and BMP2.

XPS analysis further validated coating modification and protein loading effects: the N1s peak binding energy intensity gradually increases from LDH/ZK60 to BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60 (Figures. 2p and 2q). Peak deconvolution of the N1s spectra reveals distinct chemical states: for LDH/ZK60, a single peak at 397–400 eV is assigned to nitrate (NO₃⁻) intercalated in the LDH structure (Figure. S1a) [40]; for PDA-LDH/ZK60, two peaks at ∼398.5 eV and ∼400.0 eV correspond to C–N and N–H bonds from the PDA coating, respectively (Figure. S1b) [52]; and for BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60, an additional peak at ∼401.5 eV is attributed to guanidinium groups (–C(=NH)NH₂) from the arginine residues in BMP2, confirming successful protein immobilization (Figure. S1c). XRD patterns (Figure. 2r) show Mg and MgO diffraction peaks in all scaffolds; MgO in ZK60 stems from storage oxidation, in MAO represents successful coating preparation, and in LDH-modified scaffolds indicates MAO surface coverage by LDH. Characteristic LDH diffraction peaks at 2θ = 11.14 ° and 22.32 °, indexed to the (003) and (006) planes (PDF#65–4596), are observed in LDH/ZK60, PDA-LDH/ZK60 and BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60, confirming successful LDH coating fabrication and surface coverage [53,54].

AFM analysis showed that the MAO group scaffolds exhibited the highest surface roughness, while the roughness of the scaffolds after hydrothermal treatment was significantly reduced (Figure. S2).Contact angle measurements ( Figure. 3a) show the untreated ZK60 scaffold has an average contact angle of 95.74 ± 1.26°, while the MAO scaffold decreases to 36.53 ± 3.13° due to microporous structure. LDH coating coverage increases the contact angle of LDH/ZK60–58.66 ± 4.33°, and the excellent hydrophilicity of PDA further reduces the contact angles of PDA-LDH/ZK60 and BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60–45.42 ± 3.08° and 39.34 ± 2.46°, respectively. Collectively, surface-modified coatings significantly improve scaffold hydrophilicity compared with pristine ZK60.

Nano-scratch tests (Figures. 3b and S3) show the bonding strength of MAO coating with Mg substrate is 6.09 ± 0.05 N (derived from mechanical interlocking during plasma discharge). The bonding strengths of LDH/ZK60, PDA-LDH/ZK60 and BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60 coatings reach 9.05 ± 0.27 N, 10.39 ± 0.30 N and 9.96 ± 0.32 N, respectively, which are significantly higher than MAO, as hydrothermal modification effectively enhances coating-substrate adhesion.

Moreover, nanoindentation tests shown in Figure. 3c revealed that the elastic moduli of the ZK60, MAO, LDH/ZK60, PDA-LDH/ZK60, and BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60 scaffolds were 44.74 ± 1.37 GPa, 40.82 ± 3.36 GPa, 34.70 ± 3.39 GPa, 37.36 ± 5.18 GPa, and 26.38 ± 3.49 GPa, respectively. The decrease in elastic modulus after hydrothermal treatment indicates that the structure of the surface coating closely influences the material’s elastic properties.

Protein adhesion experiments shown in Figure. 3d demonstrated significant differences in protein adsorption among the groups. The order of protein adsorption was: PDA-LDH/ZK60 > BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60 > LDH/ZK60 > MAO > ZK60.

Figure. 3e presents the in vitro release kinetics of BMP2. The results showed a cumulative release of 6.54 ± 0.24 ng/mL over 21 days, exhibiting an initial rapid release followed by a sustained slow release profile.

To investigate the corrosion resistance of the composite coatings, electrochemical tests and immersion experiments were conducted. The potentiodynamic polarization curves and the results determined by the method of intersection of the cathodic branch of the polarization curve with corrosion potential in Figure. 4a and Table 1 show that the current densities of LDH/ZK60, PDA-LDH/ZK60, and BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60 are significantly lower than those of MAO and ZK60. This indicates that LDH forms a physical protective barrier by orderly stacking layered structures that seal the micro-arc oxidation micropores. Meanwhile, the interlayer anion exchangeability adsorbs and fixes corrosive ions, and this dual mechanism synergistically enhances corrosion resistance. Figures. 4b and 4c presents the Nyquist plots and corresponding Bode plots of the scaffolds; the diameter of the capacitive arcs is closely related to the corrosion rate, with larger diameters indicating better corrosion resistance. All five groups exhibit Nyquist plots with dual capacitive arcs (Figure. 4b), and the corresponding Bode phase angle plots show two time constants (Figures. 4c), suggesting the presence of a double-layer structure.For ZK60, the double-layer structure consists of the substrate and a passivation film formed by contact between the substrate and SBF solution. PDA-LDH/ZK60 shows the largest capacitive arc diameter and the highest low-frequency (0.1 Hz) impedance modulus, indicating that PDA fills coating pores and enhances interfacial bonding stability, further improving corrosion resistance. The equivalent circuit used for analysis is shown in Figure. 4d (Rs is solution resistance, Rct is charge transfer resistance, CPEdl consists of double-layer capacitance Cd and dispersion index nd, CPEf consists of film capacitance Cf and dispersion index nf, and Rf is passivation film resistance). This equivalent electrical circuit has been widely employed and verified for evaluating the corrosion behavior of plasma electrolytic oxidation and composite coatings on magnesium alloys, as reported in recent studies [55−58]. The fitting data in Table 2 reveal that the corrosion resistance-related Rct values decrease in the order PDA-LDH/ZK60 > BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60 > LDH/ZK60 > MAO > ZK60, consistent with the trends in current density observed in the potentiodynamic polarization curves. Notably, PDA-LDH/ZK60 exhibits the largest passivation film resistance (Rf = 1409 Ω·cm²) and the lowest film capacitance (Cf = 2.695 μF·cm⁻²), further confirming the formation of a compact and protective coating.

Immersion experiments evaluated material biodegradation performance: Figures. 4e and 4f show ZK60 has the highest Mg²⁺ release and pH rise, while LDH/ZK60, PDA-LDH/ZK60, and BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60 groups show no significant Mg²⁺ release difference in early immersion. All groups exhibit "initial active degradation followed by gradual stabilization": rapid Mg²⁺ release and sharp pH rise in early stage, and slowed degradation due to corrosion product deposition and passivation film formation on material surface.Figure. S4 shows that the ZK60 group suffered severe corrosion at the early immersion stage and exhibited significant surface damage after 21 days. The MAO coating provided limited protection, with corrosion occurring from the third day. In contrast, the LDH-coated groups showed excellent corrosion resistance with no obvious surface damage during immersion. After 21 days of immersion, SEM observations (Figure. S5) revealed distinct surface morphologies. Uncoated ZK60 and MAO-coated scaffolds displayed a "dry riverbed morphology" with extensive, wide cracks (Figures. S5a, b, f, g). In the MAO group, pits from micro-arc oxidation discharge were visible, indicating severe coating degradation and substrate exposure (Figure. S5g). In contrast, LDH/ZK60, PDA-LDH/ZK60, and BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60 showed only sparse, narrower microcracks (Figures. S5c, d and e). Notably, the LDH nanosheet structure remained intact in these groups, with corrosion products deposited within the nanosheets to act as an additional sealant, providing secondary corrosion protection (Figures. S5h, i and j). Corrosion rate analysis (Figure. 4g) confirms PDA-LDH/ZK60 has the optimal corrosion resistance (1.43 ± 0.12 mm·year⁻¹). This trend is consistent with the corrosion rate Pi calculated from the potentiodynamic polarization test, where PDA-LDH/ZK60 exhibits the lowest value of 0.09 mm·year⁻¹ .

To evaluate the cellular compatibility of the materials, BMSCs were seeded on scaffolds, and live-dead staining (Figure. 5a and S7a) showed ZK60 and MAO groups had fewer adherent cells and higher dead cell ratio, while the LDH/ZK60, PDA-LDH/ZK60, and BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60 groups presented abundant live cells. To further clarify the mechanism of cytotoxicity, BMSCs were cultured with material extracts, followed by live-dead staining and flow cytometry analysis ( Figure. 5b). The gating strategy is shown in Figure S6. The results revealed that live cell proportion exceeding 80% in all groups, indicating ZK60 and MAO cytotoxicity originates from corrosion-induced bubbles (interfering cell adhesion) and localized high pH environment, rather than intrinsic elemental toxicity. Hemolysis rates of all scaffolds were below 5% (Figure. S7b), confirming good biocompatibility; subsequent experiments focused on LDH/ZK60, PDA-LDH/ZK60 and BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60.

Fluorescence staining was used to assess the spreading of BMSCs on the material surfaces. As shown in Figures. 5c、5d and S7c, after 4 and 24 h of culture, the BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60 group exhibited the largest cell spreading area, while the LDH/ZK60 group showed the smallest.

To evaluate the effects of LDH/ZK60, PDA-LDH/ZK60, and BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60 scaffolds on cell proliferation and migration, BMSCs were cultured with material extracts. The cell proliferation results in Figure. S8a demonstrated a time-dependent growth trend in all groups. PDA-LDH/ZK60 and BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60 exhibited significantly enhanced proliferation at day 7, with BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60 having the highest activity. Transwell and scratch assays (Figures. S8b-e) confirmed BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60 most prominently promoted BMSC migration.

BMSCs were treated with three scaffolds extracts, and ALP/ARS staining at 7/14 days ( Figures. 6a and 6b) showed BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60 had significantly higher ALP positive intensity and calcium nodule area, indicating enhanced osteogenic differentiation. Quantitative analysis (Figures. 6c and 6d) confirmed higher ALP activity and calcium deposition in BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60, proving BMP2 loading markedly strengthens osteogenic mineralization induction. RT-PCR (Figures. 6e-g) showed Col1a1, OPN and RUNX2 (osteogenic genes) were significantly upregulated in BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60, while no significant differences in Col1a1 and RUNX2 expression were observed between the LDH/ZK60 and PDA-LDH/ZK60 groups,verifying sustained BMP2 release promotes osteogenic differentiation. Western blot (Figures. 6h-k) further confirmed increased protein expression of the three genes in BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60, collectively demonstrating the scaffold enhances osteogenic differentiation and related gene/protein expression.

Eight weeks after implantation, micro-CT was used to analyze bone formation around the scaffolds ( Figures. 7a and 7b). CT images and three-dimensional reconstructions revealed abundant new bone tissue formation in the LDH/ZK60, PDA-LDH/ZK60, and BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60 groups (Figures. 7f-h). Quantitative analysis (BS/TV, BV/TV, BMD) with LDH/ZK60 as control showed BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60 had the highest BS/TV and BV/TV values (Figures. 7c and 7d). Specifically, the BMD value of the BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60 group was 454.25 ± 31.41 mg/cm³ , which was 1.44 times higher than that of the LDH/ZK60 group, confirming superior osteogenic performance of BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60, consistent with in vitro results.

To evaluate early new bone formation around the scaffold, fluorescence staining was performed using calcein to label mineralized deposition areas at 2 weeks post-surgery. As shown in Figures. 7i and S9a, no obvious new bone formation was observed around the LDH/ZK60 group scaffold at 2 weeks post-operation, whereas both the PDA-LDH/ZK60 group and the BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60 group showed new bone formation. Among them, the BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60 group exhibited the most significant early osteogenesis effect.

Subsequently, histological evaluation was conducted using toluidine blue-basic fuchsin staining (Figure. 7j). The results indicated mild degradation at the scaffold edges in all three groups: LDH/ZK60, PDA-LDH/ZK60, and BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60, with new bone tissue formation observed around them. Notably, the BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60 group showed the most pronounced bone formation, with new bone tissue closely growing along the degraded scaffold. Quantitative analysis of the histological sections revealed that the new bone area of the BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60 group was 1.52 ± 0.08 mm², and the bone-implant contact (BIC) rate reached 91.34 ± 1.36% (Figures. S9b and c).

Moreover, HE staining of SD rat heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney (Figure. S10) showed no abnormal morphology vs normal rats, confirming the scaffold has no visceral toxicity and excellent biocompatibility.

ZK60 magnesium alloy has been extensively investigated for its human bone-matched mechanical properties and high strength, yet its rapid degradation in physiological environments induces "bone regeneration lagging behind material degradation", resulting in premature loss of mechanical support and thus severely hindering its clinical translation [9,59]. Ideal bone implant scaffolds should satisfy dual demands of controllable degradation and efficient osteogenesis. Given the challenge of single surface modification in balancing these requirements, constructing a multilayer synergistic functional coating system has emerged as a core strategy to resolve this contradiction [7,31,43].

Electrochemical and immersion corrosion tests verified that the LDH coating mitigates corrosion via a dual mechanism: its layered stacked structure blocks corrosive medium penetration, and interlayer anion exchange adsorbs and immobilizes Cl⁻ and other corrosive ions, thereby remarkably enhancing corrosion resistance. PDA further optimizes interfacial bonding and coating integrity by infiltrating and filling nanogaps between LDH nanosheets and MAO/LDH interfacial defects to improve coating compactness [31]. The bonding strength between the coating and substrate is a key mechanical property determining service life [60]. Nano-scratch tests revealed that the bonding strengths of LDH/ZK60, PDA-LDH/ZK60 and BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60 coatings were significantly higher than the MAO group, confirming hydrothermal modification strengthens coating stability through densification and interfacial optimization, laying structural foundation for long-term corrosion protection. The PDA-LDH/ZK60 group exhibited the highest bonding strength (10.39 ± 0.30 N), verifying PDA’s "molecular glue" effect in reinforcing multilayer and substrate-coating interfaces, which suppresses coating delamination/cracking during service to ensure stable protection. Electrochemical tests and immersion corrosion experiments yielded consistent results: the PDA-LDH/ZK60 group showed the highest charge transfer resistance (3989 Ω·cm²), lowest corrosion current density (4.12 × 10⁻⁶ A·cm⁻²), and lowest immersion corrosion rate (1.43 ± 0.12 mm·year⁻¹), consistent with theoretical expectations. It is noteworthy that the corrosion resistance of the BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60 group was slightly lower than that of the PDA-LDH/ZK60 group (may be due to slight surface loosening and partial PDA dissolution caused by BMP2 immersion loading), but still superior to the LDH/ZK60 group.The slight decrease in corrosion resistance does not compromise the structural stability and biosafety required for in vitro experiments.

Material biocompatibility depends on cell-surface interactions, where hydrophilicity and protein adsorption capacity are pivotal for cell adhesion and proliferation [61,62]. Contact angle measurements showed PDA modification improved coating hydrophilicity (PDA-LDH/ZK60: 45.42 ± 3.08 °) via abundant hydroxyl/amino groups enhancing surface polarity; further BMP2 loading reduced the contact angle of BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60–39.34 ± 2.46 °, creating a favorable microenvironment for cell adhesion. Kim et al. reported that BMP2 could be directly loaded onto the surface of MAO-hydrothermally treated LDH coatings on magnesium alloys via physical adsorption [43]. In contrast, the present study introduced a PDA interlayer between the LDH coating and BMP2, which endows the system with superior protein adsorption and controlled BMP2 release compared to the direct loading strategy. Protein adsorption assays demonstrated that PDA-LDH/ZK60 and BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60 exhibited significantly higher protein adsorption than other groups, which is attributed to the synergism of PDA active groups and the high specific surface area of LDH. This synergism not only provides abundant protein anchoring sites to boost biocompatibility, but also captures endogenous osteogenic factors in the cellular microenvironment for local enrichment and stable immobilization, constructing a sustained osteoinductive microenvironment at the implantation site to support osteogenic cell adhesion, proliferation and differentiation, and ultimately accelerate bone regeneration.

Cell compatibility assays showed ZK60 and MAO groups exhibited evident cytotoxicity in direct contact culture, while their extract cell viability exceeded 80%, indicating toxicity originated from corrosion-induced bubbles interfering with cell adhesion and local high pH disrupting the cellular microenvironment, rather than elemental release [63,64]. During direct culture, the pH values of ZK60 and MAO groups reached 10–12 (Fig. 4f), causing severe cytotoxicity. In contrast, the extracts were diluted to 50% working concentration with complete medium, which reduced the pH and resulted in higher cell viability. LDH/ZK60, PDA-LDH/ZK60 and BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60 effectively regulated corrosion to reduce cytotoxicity, with BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60 showing optimal cell spreading and proliferation. Transwell and scratch tests confirmed BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60 extracts significantly promoted BMSC migration, laying a cellular foundation for bone regeneration. This advantage stems from PDA’s multi-regulation effects: scavenging free radicals to alleviate oxidative stress [31], enhancing corrosion resistance to reduce extract pH, and acting as a carrier for BMP2 loading and sustained release to optimize cellular functions.

Osteogenic differentiation is the core process of bone regeneration, involving ALP activity, mineralization capacity, and the coordinated expression of osteogenesis-related genes and proteins. In vitro osteogenic experiments in this study showed that the BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60 group exhibited the highest ALP activity at the early differentiation stage (7 days) and the most significant mineralized nodule formation at the late stage (14 days). RT-PCR and Western blot results confirmed that the expression of key osteogenic genes and proteins such as Col1a1, OPN, and RUNX2 were upregulated in this group, verifying that the sustained release of active BMP2 from the coating is the core driver of osteogenic differentiation. As a potent osteoinductive factor, BMP2 can directly bind to BMP receptors on the cell membrane, activate the Smad signaling pathway, and upregulate osteogenesis-related gene expression, accelerating differentiation [65,66].

Animal experiments further validated the in vivo osteogenic efficacy of the composite coating. Micro-CT analysis showed that the BS/TV, BV/TV ratios, and BMD of the BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60 group were significantly higher than those of the control group.Fluorescence labeling and histological analyses confirmed that this group exhibited obvious new bone formation as early as 2 weeks post-operation, and at 8 weeks post-operation, the new bone tissue grew tightly along the degraded scaffold, with the maximum volume of newly formed bone and the highest bone-implant contact, effectively addressing the critical issue of "bone regeneration lagging behind material degradation". This result is highly consistent with in vitro experiments, indicating that the osteogenic enhancement mechanism of the composite coating can effectively function both in vivo and in vitro—by regulating degradation rate and sustained BMP2 release to achieve rate matching between "material degradation" and "bone regeneration," maintaining mechanical support during the critical bone healing period and accelerating bone integration through osteoinductive effects, thereby significantly improving bone repair outcomes. Meanwhile, histopathological analysis of major organs showed no abnormalities, and combined with in vitro hemolysis rates below 5%, fully demonstrating the good in vivo and in vitro biosafety of this composite coating system.

To understand the role of composite coatings, a possible anticorrosion mechanism is proposed in Figure. S11. For bare ZK60 (Figures. S11a and f), the substrate is directly exposed to the corrosive medium, where Cl⁻ ions rapidly induce pitting corrosion. The MAO coating (Figures. S11b and g) provides a basic physical barrier, but its internal cavities still allow Cl⁻ penetration, resulting in insufficient protection.In contrast, the LDH-based composite coatings exhibit a synergistic protective effect.First, the LDH layer (Figures. S11c and h) acts as an efficient ion-exchange barrier, capturing Cl⁻ and releasing intercalated NO₃⁻ inhibitors, which effectively slows down the corrosion process.Second, the dense PDA interlayer (Figures. S11d and i) further seals the porous LDH coating, reducing Cl⁻ diffusion and enhancing the barrier performance.Finally, the topmost BMP2 layer (Figures. S11e and j) endows the material with excellent bioactivity. This multi-layered structure provides superior corrosion protection and effectively inhibits the corrosion of the ZK60 substrate.

However, this study has several limitations. First, the sample size in animal experiments was relatively small and a blank control group was lacking. Second, only a single timepoint (8 weeks) was used for Micro-CT analysis, without multiple time-point evaluations. In addition, this study focused on bone regeneration evaluation at 8 weeks post-operation and has not systematically tracked the long-term (e.g., over 6 months) degradation behavior, mechanical support stability, and long-term bone integration effects of the material. Future studies should extend the animal experiment duration to clarify the degradation-regeneration matching and mechanical attenuation patterns of the coating during the late bone healing stage.

This study successfully developed a multifunctional BMP2@PDA-LDH/ZK60 composite coating. The coating features a MAO layer that provides a robust substrate, an LDH layer that achieves dense sealing and anion exchange, and a PDA layer that enhances the interface, significantly improving the corrosion resistance of the ZK60 magnesium alloy. Meanwhile, the stable loading and sustained release of BMP2 enhance the osteogenic bioactivity of the material. Both in vitro and in vivo experiments consistently demonstrated that this composite coating not only improves the corrosion resistance of the magnesium alloy but also effectively promotes bone defect regeneration. This study offers a novel strategy for biodegradable magnesium alloys with excellent corrosion resistance and biocompatibility.
